  "internal_reasoning": "The work shown appears to be a contemporary installation consisting of two vertically oriented digital screens or monitor-based lightboxes displaying close-up, highly stylized female faces, mounted side by side above a mid-century-style cabinet or console with visible power cords. Because I have no artist, title, date, edition, medium specification, exhibition history, or provenance, the valuation must be based purely on what is visible and on how anonymous contemporary digital/video installation works of this apparent quality tend to perform in the market. That lack of identifying information is the single greatest suppressor of value. If this were by a recognized blue-chip practitioner of video, post-internet, or digital portraiture, the valuation could rise dramatically; absent attribution, the market treats it much more cautiously.\n\nArtistically, the installation has a polished, atmospheric presentation. The two-screen arrangement creates a diptych structure, and the softly blurred, sensuous faces suggest influences from fashion imagery, AI-derived portraiture, manipulated photography, or digitally painted figuration. The chromatic handling—violets, pinks, and smoky shadows—produces an immersive, cinematic mood. The pairing of the faces introduces a conversation around duplication, identity, glamour, mediation, and perhaps synthetic beauty. There is enough visual resolution and finish to suggest a professionally produced work rather than an amateur object. However, the imagery itself, while striking, does not obviously evidence a singular, historically important hand or a distinctly innovative formal language from the image alone. It sits comfortably within a broad field of current market-familiar digital portrait aesthetics.\n\nFrom a medium standpoint, the work seems to rely on electronic display hardware as an intrinsic component. That can support value when the artist is established, but for unconfirmed authorship it can also constrain price because buyers factor in technological obsolescence, maintenance, editioning uncertainty, file integrity, playback format, and replacement protocol for screens. Major auction houses generally require precise information for media works: certificate, edition size, file format, display instructions, and rights to exhibit. None of that is available here. Without those assurances, the work would likely be catalogued conservatively, perhaps as a decorative contemporary media installation rather than a high-conviction fine-art lot.\n\nCondition, as far as can be judged, appears visually good. The screens are functioning; no obvious dead pixels, major scratches, or structural damage are apparent. The installation environment is clean and professional. Still, condition assessment for media art is limited from a single image. One cannot inspect burn-in, color drift, sound components if any, software stability, or whether the cords and cabinet are original to the piece. The furniture element may or may not be integral. If integral, it slightly enhances sculptural presence; if incidental, it does little for value.\n\nComparable market territory, assuming no recognized artist attribution, would include anonymous or lesser-known contemporary digital installations, editioned video portraits, and design-forward media works sold through regional contemporary sales, online auctions, or galleries rather than top evening sales. Such works often trade from low thousands into the low tens of thousands depending on scale, production values, and whether they are unique or editioned. A two-screen installation of this apparent sophistication might reasonably attract interest around $5,000 to $15,000 if catalogued well and offered with complete technical components. At a major international auction, where attribution and market confidence matter heavily, the absence of authorship and documentation would likely keep expectations toward the lower-middle of that band.\n\nBalancing the work’s strong visual appeal and contemporary relevance against the very substantial penalties for anonymity, uncertain medium details, and unknown edition/provenance status, I arrive at a fair market value estimate of $8,000. That figure reflects what a motivated buyer might realistically pay today for an attractive, professionally realized but unattributed contemporary digital diptych installation in working order.",

    "Visually polished contemporary two-screen digital installation with strong decorative and atmospheric appeal",

parsed.internal_reasoning:
The work shown appears to be a contemporary installation consisting of two vertically oriented digital screens or monitor-based lightboxes displaying close-up, highly stylized female faces, mounted side by side above a mid-century-style cabinet or console with visible power cords. Because I have no artist, title, date, edition, medium specification, exhibition history, or provenance, the valuation must be based purely on what is visible and on how anonymous contemporary digital/video installation works of this apparent quality tend to perform in the market. That lack of identifying information is the single greatest suppressor of value. If this were by a recognized blue-chip practitioner of video, post-internet, or digital portraiture, the valuation could rise dramatically; absent attribution, the market treats it much more cautiously.

Artistically, the installation has a polished, atmospheric presentation. The two-screen arrangement creates a diptych structure, and the softly blurred, sensuous faces suggest influences from fashion imagery, AI-derived portraiture, manipulated photography, or digitally painted figuration. The chromatic handling—violets, pinks, and smoky shadows—produces an immersive, cinematic mood. The pairing of the faces introduces a conversation around duplication, identity, glamour, mediation, and perhaps synthetic beauty. There is enough visual resolution and finish to suggest a professionally produced work rather than an amateur object. However, the imagery itself, while striking, does not obviously evidence a singular, historically important hand or a distinctly innovative formal language from the image alone. It sits comfortably within a broad field of current market-familiar digital portrait aesthetics.

From a medium standpoint, the work seems to rely on electronic display hardware as an intrinsic component. That can support value when the artist is established, but for unconfirmed authorship it can also constrain price because buyers factor in technological obsolescence, maintenance, editioning uncertainty, file integrity, playback format, and replacement protocol for screens. Major auction houses generally require precise information for media works: certificate, edition size, file format, display instructions, and rights to exhibit. None of that is available here. Without those assurances, the work would likely be catalogued conservatively, perhaps as a decorative contemporary media installation rather than a high-conviction fine-art lot.

Condition, as far as can be judged, appears visually good. The screens are functioning; no obvious dead pixels, major scratches, or structural damage are apparent. The installation environment is clean and professional. Still, condition assessment for media art is limited from a single image. One cannot inspect burn-in, color drift, sound components if any, software stability, or whether the cords and cabinet are original to the piece. The furniture element may or may not be integral. If integral, it slightly enhances sculptural presence; if incidental, it does little for value.

Comparable market territory, assuming no recognized artist attribution, would include anonymous or lesser-known contemporary digital installations, editioned video portraits, and design-forward media works sold through regional contemporary sales, online auctions, or galleries rather than top evening sales. Such works often trade from low thousands into the low tens of thousands depending on scale, production values, and whether they are unique or editioned. A two-screen installation of this apparent sophistication might reasonably attract interest around $5,000 to $15,000 if catalogued well and offered with complete technical components. At a major international auction, where attribution and market confidence matter heavily, the absence of authorship and documentation would likely keep expectations toward the lower-middle of that band.

Balancing the work’s strong visual appeal and contemporary relevance against the very substantial penalties for anonymity, uncertain medium details, and unknown edition/provenance status, I arrive at a fair market value estimate of $8,000. That figure reflects what a motivated buyer might realistically pay today for an attractive, professionally realized but unattributed contemporary digital diptych installation in working order.
key_factors: Visually polished contemporary two-screen digital installation with strong decorative and atmospheric appeal
No artist attribution, title, date, provenance, or edition information, severely limiting auction confidence
Electronic media works carry maintenance, obsolescence, and documentation risks that suppress value
Condition appears generally good from the image, with functioning displays and professional presentation
Comparable anonymous or lesser-known contemporary digital installations typically trade in the low-thousands to low-five-figure range
